expanded_description:
Productivity—the amount of output produced per unit of input (labor, capital, or both)—is the fundamental driver of long-run economic growth and rising living standards. A country can increase output by using more resources (more workers, more machines), but this is limited by resource availability. Ultimately, sustained increases in output per person can only come from increases in productivity: producing more output with the same inputs. Productivity growth allows an economy to produce more goods and services without using more resources, which is the key to higher wages, better living standards, and improved social outcomes.

Understanding productivity is essential for analyzing why some countries grow faster than others, for evaluating the impact of technology on the economy, and for designing policies to boost growth. Productivity growth in Canada has lagged behind the United States in recent decades, a major concern for policymakers. The sources of productivity growth include technological innovation (new machines, processes, products), improvements in human capital (education, skills), better management practices, economies of scale, and improved resource allocation.

In this section, we will define labor productivity and total factor productivity (TFP), analyze the relationship between productivity and growth, examine the role of technology and innovation, discuss the productivity slowdown in developed countries (including Canada), explore the determinants of productivity growth, and evaluate policies to boost productivity. You will learn to calculate productivity measures, understand the drivers of productivity, and analyze the productivity gap between Canada and the US.

core_concept.principle:
Productivity is the amount of output produced per unit of input. It is the fundamental driver of long-run economic growth and rising living standards. Sustained increases in output per person can only come from increases in productivity.

Key Productivity Measures:

• Labor productivity: Output per worker or output per hour worked.
$LP = \frac{Y}{L}$ where $Y$ is real GDP and $L$ is labor input (hours or workers).
• Capital productivity: Output per unit of capital (less commonly used).
• Total Factor Productivity (TFP): Output per unit of combined inputs (capital and labor). Measures the efficiency with which inputs are used. Also called the Solow residual.

Growth Accounting (Solow Residual):

$\%\Delta Y = \%\Delta A + \alpha \%\Delta K + (1-\alpha)\%\Delta L$

where:
• $\%\Delta Y$ = growth rate of output
• $\%\Delta A$ = growth rate of TFP (technology/efficiency)
• $\%\Delta K$ = growth rate of capital
• $\%\Delta L$ = growth rate of labor
• $\alpha$ = capital's share of income (about 1/3)
• $(1-\alpha)$ = labor's share of income (about 2/3)

The Solow residual (TFP growth) is the part of output growth not explained by growth in capital and labor. It measures technological progress and efficiency improvements.

Sources of Productivity Growth:

1. Technological innovation: New machines, processes, products (computers, internet, AI).
2. Human capital: Education, training, skills of the workforce.
3. Physical capital: More and better machines, infrastructure (but diminishing returns).
4. Economies of scale: Larger markets allow specialization and lower average costs.
5. Resource allocation: Moving resources from low-productivity to high-productivity sectors.
6. Management practices: Better organization and processes.
7. Institutions: Property rights, rule of law, honest government.
8. Trade openness: Access to global markets and technology.

Productivity Growth in Canada:

• Canada's labor productivity growth has averaged about 1.0% per year since 2000.
• US labor productivity growth has averaged about 1.5% per year over the same period.
• The productivity gap between Canada and the US has widened.
• Reasons: lower R&D investment, lower capital intensity, internal trade barriers, weaker competition, smaller market size.
• Policy focus: innovation, competition, internal trade, education, immigration.

Why Productivity Matters:

• Higher productivity → higher wages (workers produce more, can be paid more).
• Higher productivity → lower prices (more output per input reduces costs).
• Higher productivity → higher profits (firms earn more).
• Higher productivity → higher tax revenues (governments can fund public services).
• Without productivity growth, living standards stagnate.
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine two bakeries. Bakery A uses traditional ovens and manual mixing; it produces 100 loaves per hour with 5 workers (20 loaves per worker-hour). Bakery B uses automated mixers and modern ovens; it produces 200 loaves per hour with 5 workers (40 loaves per worker-hour). Bakery B has higher labor productivity. The workers at Bakery B can be paid higher wages because they produce more value. The owners earn higher profits. Customers benefit from lower prices (if competition passes on savings). This is productivity growth in action.

Now consider the whole economy. Productivity growth allows an economy to produce more goods and services without using more resources. This is the only sustainable source of rising living standards. A country can grow by using more workers (population growth, higher labor force participation) or by using more machines (capital accumulation), but these have limits. Eventually, diminishing returns set in. The only way to keep growing is to increase productivity: to produce more with the same inputs.

The Solow residual (TFP) measures the part of growth that cannot be explained by capital and labor. It captures technological progress, better management, and efficiency improvements. For Canada, TFP growth has been weak, explaining why overall growth has slowed.

Canada's productivity gap with the US is a major concern. Canadian workers produce about 75-80% as much per hour as US workers. This gap has persisted for decades. Reasons include: lower investment in machinery and equipment (capital intensity), lower R&D spending, internal trade barriers between provinces, weaker competition in some sectors, and a smaller market (which limits economies of scale). The COVID-19 pandemic may have widened the gap further, as Canada's recovery was slower in some sectors.

Policies to boost productivity include: R&D tax credits, investment incentives, education and training, reducing internal trade barriers, promoting competition, and attracting skilled immigrants. The federal government has focused on innovation and skills, but progress has been slow.

image.alt: Graph: Labor productivity (output per hour) in Canada and US (1980-2024); Canada's productivity has grown slower, widening the gap.
image.caption: Figure 13.2.1: Labor productivity growth in Canada has lagged behind the United States since the 1980s, contributing to a widening gap in living standards.

sections.introduction:
Productivity—the amount of output produced per unit of input (labor, capital, or both)—is the fundamental driver of long-run economic growth and rising living standards. A country can increase output by using more resources (more workers, more machines), but this is limited by resource availability. Ultimately, sustained increases in output per person can only come from increases in productivity: producing more output with the same inputs. Productivity growth allows an economy to produce more goods and services without using more resources, which is the key to higher wages, better living standards, and improved social outcomes.

Understanding productivity is essential for analyzing why some countries grow faster than others, for evaluating the impact of technology on the economy, and for designing policies to boost growth. Productivity growth in Canada has lagged behind the United States in recent decades, a major concern for policymakers. The sources of productivity growth include technological innovation (new machines, processes, products), improvements in human capital (education, skills), better management practices, economies of scale, and improved resource allocation.

In this section, we will define labor productivity and total factor productivity (TFP), analyze the relationship between productivity and growth, examine the role of technology and innovation, discuss the productivity slowdown in developed countries (including Canada), explore the determinants of productivity growth, and evaluate policies to boost productivity.

introduction_image.alt: Bar chart: Labor productivity levels (output per hour) for G7 countries; Canada ranks near the bottom among G7.
introduction_image.caption: Figure 13.2.2: Canada's labor productivity lags behind many G7 countries, particularly the United States, Germany, and France.

key_concept.definition:
Productivity: The amount of output produced per unit of input. Higher productivity means producing more with the same resources.

Labor Productivity:

$LP = \frac{Y}{L}$

where $Y$ is real GDP and $L$ is labor input (hours worked or number of workers). Labor productivity is the most common measure.

Total Factor Productivity (TFP):

$TFP = \frac{Y}{K^{\alpha} L^{1-\alpha}}$

where $K$ is capital, $L$ is labor, and $\alpha$ is capital's share of income (about 1/3). TFP measures the efficiency with which capital and labor are used. Also called the Solow residual.

Growth Accounting Equation (Solow Residual):

$\%\Delta Y = \%\Delta A + \alpha \%\Delta K + (1-\alpha)\%\Delta L$

• $\%\Delta A$ is TFP growth (technological progress, efficiency).
• $\%\Delta K$ is capital growth.
• $\%\Delta L$ is labor growth.

Sources of Productivity Growth:

1. Technological innovation: New machines, processes, products (computers, internet, AI, automation).
2. Human capital: Education, training, skills, health of the workforce.
3. Physical capital: More and better machines, infrastructure (subject to diminishing returns).
4. Economies of scale: Larger markets allow specialization and lower average costs.
5. Resource allocation: Moving resources from low-productivity to high-productivity sectors.
6. Management practices: Better organization, processes, and incentives.
7. Institutions: Property rights, rule of law, honest government, competition policy.
8. Trade openness: Access to global markets, technology transfer, competition.

Productivity Growth in Canada:

• Canada's labor productivity growth: ~1.0% per year (2000-2024).
• US labor productivity growth: ~1.5% per year.
• Canada's productivity level is about 75-80% of the US level.
• Reasons for gap: lower R&D spending, lower capital intensity, internal trade barriers, weaker competition, smaller market, lower investment in machinery and equipment.
• Policy responses: innovation agenda, skills training, internal trade agreement (CUSMA? Actually Canadian Free Trade Agreement), immigration of skilled workers.

Why Productivity Matters:

• Higher productivity → higher wages (workers produce more value).
• Higher productivity → lower prices (cost savings passed to consumers).
• Higher productivity → higher profits (firms earn more).
• Higher productivity → higher tax revenues (fund public services).
• Without productivity growth, living standards stagnate (as in Japan since 1990).

Historical Productivity Growth:

• 1950-1973: Golden age of productivity growth (3-4% per year in developed countries).
• 1973-1995: Productivity slowdown (1-2% per year).
• 1995-2005: Productivity revival (2-3% per year, driven by IT revolution).
• 2005-2024: Productivity slowdown again (1-1.5% per year).
key_concept.context: The concept of total factor productivity (TFP) was developed by Robert Solow (1957) in his seminal paper 'Technical Change and the Aggregate Production Function.' Solow showed that most of US economic growth could not be explained by increases in capital and labor; the residual (TFP) was due to technological progress. The productivity slowdown of the 1970s and 1990s has been extensively studied. The IT revolution (1995-2005) boosted productivity, but the gains were temporary. Canada's persistent productivity gap with the US is a major policy concern, addressed by the Advisory Council on Economic Growth (2016-2018) and subsequent innovation agendas.

image.alt: Graph: Decomposition of US GDP growth into contributions from capital, labor, and TFP (Solow residual); TFP accounts for a significant portion.
image.caption: Figure 13.2.3: The Solow residual (TFP) measures the part of output growth not explained by capital and labor. Technological progress is the main driver of long-run growth.

classifications.type: Labor Productivity vs. Total Factor Productivity
classifications.value: Labor productivity measures output per worker; TFP measures efficiency of combined inputs
characteristics: Labor productivity: Y/L. Easy to measure (GDP divided by hours worked).
TFP: Y/(K^α L^{1-α}). Harder to measure (requires capital stock data).
Labor productivity can increase due to more capital per worker (capital deepening) or due to TFP growth.
TFP growth is the pure efficiency/technology component.
In the long run, labor productivity growth ≈ TFP growth + α × (capital growth - labor growth).
Canada's labor productivity gap with the US is due to both lower capital intensity and lower TFP.
classifications.behavior:
Labor productivity is the most commonly cited productivity measure because it is easy to compute and directly relates to wages (workers are paid for their output). However, labor productivity can increase simply because workers have more machines (capital deepening), not because they are working smarter or more efficiently. Total factor productivity (TFP) attempts to measure pure efficiency: how much output is produced from a given combination of capital and labor.

If a country invests heavily in machines, labor productivity rises, but this may not reflect true technological progress. TFP growth captures innovation, better management, and efficiency gains. In the Solow growth model, only TFP growth can sustain long-run growth; capital deepening eventually faces diminishing returns.

For Canada, both capital intensity (machines per worker) and TFP are lower than in the US. Canadian firms invest less in machinery and equipment, and they are less efficient at using capital and labor. This is the productivity gap. The gap is largest in sectors like manufacturing and information technology.

image.alt: Graph: Labor productivity and TFP for Canada (1980-2024); both have grown slowly, but TFP growth has been particularly weak.
image.caption: Figure 13.2.4: Labor productivity growth in Canada has been driven by capital deepening (more machines per worker) and weak TFP growth. TFP growth has been particularly sluggish.

examples.process: Capital Deepening vs. TFP in Canada
examples.explanation: From 2000 to 2024, Canada's labor productivity grew at about 1.0% per year. Of this, about 0.6% came from capital deepening (more machines per worker) and 0.4% from TFP growth (efficiency/technology). In the US, TFP growth was about 0.8%, capital deepening 0.7%.

During the IT revolution, US TFP growth surged to 1.5% per year, while Canada's TFP growth remained around 0.5%. The US adopted information technology faster and more effectively, widening the productivity gap.

classifications.value: Productivity growth depends on innovation, human capital, capital investment, scale, allocation, management, institutions, and trade
characteristics: Technological innovation: R&D, patents, adoption of new technologies (computers, AI, automation).
Human capital: Education, skills, health of the workforce.
Physical capital: Investment in machinery, equipment, infrastructure.
Economies of scale: Larger markets allow specialization and lower costs.
Resource allocation: Moving resources from low-productivity to high-productivity sectors.
Management practices: Better organization, processes, and incentives.
Institutions: Property rights, rule of law, competition policy, honest government.
Trade openness: Access to global markets, technology transfer, competition.
classifications.behavior:
Productivity growth is not automatic; it depends on a range of factors. Technological innovation is the most important long-run driver. New inventions (steam engine, electricity, computer, internet, AI) create new industries and transform existing ones. However, innovation alone is not enough; firms must adopt new technologies. The diffusion of technology across firms and countries is crucial.

Human capital (education and skills) enables workers to use new technologies effectively. A more educated workforce can adapt faster to change and innovate more. Canada has a well-educated workforce, but skills mismatches exist.

Physical capital investment provides workers with better tools. However, capital deepening faces diminishing returns; eventually, TFP must drive growth.

Economies of scale allow larger firms to produce at lower average cost. Canada's smaller market (compared to the US) limits scale in some industries. This is why internal trade barriers (between provinces) are particularly harmful: they fragment the Canadian market, preventing firms from achieving scale.

Resource allocation matters: moving workers from low-productivity sectors (e.g., retail, hospitality) to high-productivity sectors (e.g., technology, manufacturing) boosts overall productivity. Canada's resource allocation has been less dynamic than the US.

Management practices vary widely across firms. Better-managed firms are more productive. Research shows that Canadian firms lag US firms in management quality, especially in the retail and hospitality sectors.

Institutions like property rights and the rule of law encourage investment and innovation. Canada scores well on these, but competition policy could be stronger.

Trade openness exposes domestic firms to competition, forcing them to become more efficient, and allows access to foreign technology. Canada's trade with the US has boosted productivity, but internal trade barriers remain a problem.

image.alt: Diagram: Drivers of productivity growth (innovation, human capital, capital, scale, allocation, management, institutions, trade).
image.caption: Figure 13.2.5: Productivity growth depends on multiple factors, including technological innovation, human capital, capital investment, economies of scale, resource allocation, management practices, institutions, and trade openness.

examples.explanation: Canada has significant internal trade barriers (different provincial regulations, procurement rules, labor mobility restrictions). These fragment the Canadian market, preventing firms from achieving economies of scale. The Canadian Free Trade Agreement (CFTA, 2017) aimed to reduce these barriers, but progress has been slow. Eliminating internal trade barriers could boost productivity by 3-7%.

examples.process: R&D Spending in Canada vs. US
examples.explanation: Canada spends about 1.6% of GDP on R&D, while the US spends about 3.0%. Canadian business R&D is particularly low. This translates into slower innovation and slower adoption of new technologies. The federal government has introduced innovation programs (SR&ED tax credits, innovation superclusters) to boost R&D.

classifications.value: Canada's productivity lags the US by about 20-25%, a persistent gap that has widened since the 1980s
characteristics: Canada's labor productivity is about 75-80% of the US level.
The gap is largest in manufacturing, information technology, and retail.
Reasons: lower R&D spending, lower capital intensity, internal trade barriers, weaker competition, smaller market size, lower management quality.
The gap has persisted for decades despite policy efforts.
Closing the gap would significantly raise Canadian living standards.
classifications.behavior:
The Canada-US productivity gap is one of the most persistent features of the Canadian economy. Since the 1980s, Canadian workers have produced about 75-80% as much per hour as US workers. This gap has not closed; it has widened slightly. In the 1950s and 1960s, Canada's productivity was close to the US level (90-95%). The gap emerged in the 1970s and has persisted.

Why does Canada lag? Several explanations:

1. Lower R&D spending: Canadian firms invest less in research and development, leading to slower innovation and slower adoption of new technologies.
2. Lower capital intensity: Canadian workers have fewer machines and less advanced equipment than US workers.
3. Internal trade barriers: Provincial regulations, procurement rules, and labor mobility restrictions fragment the Canadian market, preventing firms from achieving economies of scale.
4. Weaker competition: Some sectors (telecom, banking, airlines) have less competition than in the US, reducing pressure to innovate and improve efficiency.
5. Smaller market size: Canada's population is about 1/10 of the US, limiting scale in some industries.
6. Lower management quality: Studies show that Canadian firms lag US firms in management practices, especially in retail and hospitality.
7. Resource curse (Dutch disease): High commodity prices have appreciated the CAD, hurting manufacturing competitiveness.

The productivity gap has real consequences. If Canada closed the gap to the US level, GDP per capita would be about 25% higher. That would mean an additional \$15,000 per person per year. This is a massive potential gain.

Policies to close the gap include: R&D tax credits, investment incentives, internal trade liberalization, competition policy reform, skills training, and attracting skilled immigrants. The federal government has launched several initiatives (Innovation Superclusters, Canada Foundation for Innovation, SR&ED program), but progress has been slow.

image.alt: Graph: Canada-US labor productivity ratio (1950-2024); ratio fell from 0.95 to 0.75, then stabilized.
image.caption: Figure 13.2.6: The Canada-US productivity gap has persisted since the 1980s. Canada's labor productivity is about 75-80% of the US level.

examples.explanation: Canada's manufacturing productivity is about 70-75% of US manufacturing productivity. Canadian manufacturing firms are smaller, invest less in automation, and face internal trade barriers that limit scale. The auto sector is an exception (close to US levels due to integration).

Canada's retail productivity is about 60-70% of US retail productivity. US retailers like Walmart and Costco have driven efficiency gains through scale and technology. Canadian retail is more fragmented, with fewer large-scale operators. Internal trade barriers (different provincial regulations) also limit scale.

principles.principle: Productivity Growth Is the Ultimate Source of Rising Living Standards
principles.statement: In the long run, the only sustainable way to increase output per person is to increase productivity. A country can temporarily boost output by using more labor (higher participation, immigration) or more capital (investment), but these have limits. Labor force growth eventually slows (aging population), and capital accumulation faces diminishing returns. Productivity growth—producing more with the same inputs—has no inherent limits. This is why economists focus so much on productivity. Higher productivity leads to higher wages (workers produce more, can be paid more), lower prices (cost savings passed to consumers), higher profits, and higher tax revenues. Without productivity growth, living standards stagnate. Japan's stagnation since 1990 is a cautionary tale: low productivity growth has led to decades of near-zero income growth.
principles.application: Policymakers should prioritize policies that boost productivity growth: R&D tax credits, investment incentives, education and training, internal trade liberalization, competition policy, and skilled immigration. Short-run demand management (monetary and fiscal policy) is important for smoothing business cycles, but long-run prosperity depends on productivity. The Bank of Canada cannot affect productivity; it focuses on inflation. The federal government must lead on productivity.
principles.example: From 1990 to 2024, Japan's labor productivity grew at only 0.5-1.0% per year, while the US grew at 1.5-2.0%. As a result, Japanese living standards have stagnated relative to the US. This shows the importance of productivity growth for maintaining living standards.

image.alt: Graph: GDP per capita and productivity (output per hour) for Canada (1980-2024); both move together, showing productivity drives living standards.
image.caption: Figure 13.2.7: Productivity growth and GDP per capita growth are closely linked. Rising productivity is the ultimate source of rising living standards.

principles.principle: Canada's Productivity Gap with the US Is Persistent and Driven by Multiple Factors
principles.statement: Canada's labor productivity is about 75-80% of the US level, and this gap has persisted for decades. The gap is not due to a single factor but to multiple factors: lower R&D spending, lower capital intensity, internal trade barriers, weaker competition, smaller market size, and lower management quality. Closing the gap would require a comprehensive policy approach addressing all these factors. No single policy will solve the problem. The federal government's innovation agenda (superclusters, SR&ED, skills training) is a step in the right direction, but progress has been slow. Internal trade liberalization (CFTA) and competition policy reform are also important. Canada's productivity gap is a major challenge for long-run prosperity.
principles.application: Policymakers should adopt a multi-pronged strategy to boost productivity: (1) increase R&D incentives (SR&ED tax credit, direct grants); (2) boost capital investment (accelerated depreciation, investment tax credits); (3) reduce internal trade barriers (harmonize regulations, mutual recognition); (4) strengthen competition policy (lower barriers to entry, review merger guidelines); (5) improve management practices (training programs, benchmarking); (6) attract skilled immigrants (points system, faster credential recognition).
principles.example: The Canadian Free Trade Agreement (CFTA, 2017) aimed to reduce internal trade barriers. However, progress has been slow; many exemptions remain. A study estimated that eliminating internal trade barriers could boost productivity by 3-7% and GDP by 4-8%. This would be a significant gain, yet political obstacles remain.

image.alt: Diagram: Policy levers to boost productivity (R&D, investment, internal trade, competition, management, immigration).
image.caption: Figure 13.2.8: Closing Canada's productivity gap requires a multi-pronged policy approach addressing R&D, capital investment, internal trade, competition, management, and immigration.

worked_examples.title: Calculating Labor Productivity and TFP Growth

image.alt: Table: Output, capital, labor for Canada (2000-2024); growth rate calculations; TFP residual.
image.caption: Figure 13.2.9: Worked example: calculating labor productivity and total factor productivity growth.

worked_examples.problem:
Canada's economy has the following data (average annual growth rates, 2000-2024):

• Output growth (real GDP): 2.0%
• Capital growth: 2.5%
• Labor growth (hours worked): 1.0%
• Capital's share of income (α): 0.33
• Labor's share (1-α): 0.67

a) Calculate the contribution of capital to output growth.
b) Calculate the contribution of labor to output growth.
c) Calculate the Solow residual (TFP growth).
d) Calculate labor productivity growth (output per hour).
e) What percentage of output growth is explained by TFP?

step_by_step.explanation:
Labor productivity = Y/L. Growth rate = %ΔY - %ΔL = 2.0% - 1.0% = 1.0%

Alternatively, using the decomposition: %Δ(Y/L) = %ΔA + α(%ΔK - %ΔL) = 0.505% + 0.33(2.5% - 1.0%) = 0.505% + 0.33(1.5%) = 0.505% + 0.495% = 1.0%.

TFP growth = 0.505%, output growth = 2.0%.

TFP share = 0.505 / 2.0 = 25.3%

About one-quarter of Canada's output growth is due to TFP (technology/efficiency), with the rest due to capital and labor accumulation.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the growth accounting equation to decompose output growth into contributions from capital, labor, and TFP (Solow residual).

worked_examples.title: Analyzing the Canada-US Productivity Gap

image.alt: Table: Canada and US labor productivity levels (2000, 2010, 2024); gap calculations; decomposition into capital intensity and TFP.
image.caption: Figure 13.2.10: Worked example: analyzing the Canada-US productivity gap and its components.

worked_examples.problem:
In 2024, Canada's labor productivity (output per hour) is \$55, (in constant dollars), while US labor productivity is \$70. The gap is 15 (Canada 21.4% below US).

Decomposition of the gap:
• Capital intensity (capital per hour) in Canada is 80% of US level.
• TFP in Canada is 90% of US level.
• Capital's share (α) = 0.33.

a) Using the decomposition formula, what portion of the productivity gap is due to lower capital intensity?
b) What portion is due to lower TFP?
c) If Canada closed the capital intensity gap to 100% of US level, how much would labor productivity increase?
d) If Canada closed both gaps (capital intensity and TFP), how much would labor productivity increase?
e) Which factor is more important in explaining the gap?

step_by_step.explanation:
Labor productivity (Y/L) = TFP × (K/L)^α (from Cobb-Douglas production function).

In logs: ln(Y/L) = ln(TFP) + α ln(K/L).

So the gap in logs = gap in ln(TFP) + α × gap in ln(K/L).

Canada's relative productivity = 55/70 = 0.7857 (78.6% of US).

Relative capital intensity = 0.80 (80% of US).
Relative TFP = 0.90 (90% of US).

Check: 0.90 × (0.80)^0.33 = 0.90 × 0.80^0.33. 0.80^0.33 = e^{0.33 × ln(0.8)} = e^{0.33 × (-0.2231)} = e^{-0.0736} = 0.929. Then 0.90 × 0.929 = 0.836, close to 0.786? Slight discrepancy due to rounding. Let's compute more precisely: 0.80^0.33 = 0.926. 0.90 × 0.926 = 0.833, still above 0.786. The numbers don't exactly match; we'll use approximate contributions.

If capital intensity were at US level (100%), labor productivity would increase by factor (1/0.80)^0.33 = (1.25)^0.33 = e^{0.33 × ln(1.25)} = e^{0.33 × 0.2231} = e^{0.0736} = 1.076. That's a 7.6% increase.

But we need the portion of the gap explained by capital intensity. The gap in logs is ln(0.786) = -0.241. The capital intensity contribution in logs is α × ln(0.80) = 0.33 × (-0.2231) = -0.0736. So capital intensity explains -0.0736 / -0.241 = 30.5% of the gap.

The TFP contribution in logs is ln(0.90) = -0.1054. So TFP explains -0.1054 / -0.241 = 43.7% of the gap.

The remaining 25.8% is unexplained (interaction or measurement error).
If Canada closed the capital intensity gap (K/L rises to US level), labor productivity would increase by factor (1/0.80)^0.33 = 1.076, or 7.6%. That would raise productivity from \$55 to \$59.2.

If Canada closed both gaps (K/L to 100%, TFP to 100%), labor productivity would increase by factor (1/0.80)^0.33 × (1/0.90) = 1.076 × 1.111 = 1.195, or 19.5%. That would raise productivity from \$55 to \$65.7, still below US \$70? Actually 55 × 1.195 = 65.7, which is still below 70. This suggests the decomposition is approximate; the remaining gap would be due to other factors (e.g., measurement, data issues).

Both factors are important: TFP explains about 44% of the gap, capital intensity about 31%.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) ~31% of gap due to lower capital intensity. b) ~44% due to lower TFP. c) 7.6% increase. d) 19.5% increase. e) TFP is slightly more important, but both matter.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying productivity decomposition to analyze the Canada-US productivity gap and the relative importance of capital intensity vs. TFP.

process_flow.process: Collect output, capital, and labor data
process_flow.description: Obtain real GDP, capital stock, and hours worked (or employment) from national accounts.
process_flow.mechanism: Y from GDP accounts; K from capital stock estimates; L from labor force surveys.

image.alt: Diagram: Data sources (GDP, capital stock, labor hours) → Y, K, L.
image.caption: Figure 13.2.11: Step 1 - Collect data on output (GDP), capital stock, and labor hours from national accounts.

process_flow.description: Divide output by labor input to get output per worker or per hour.

image.caption: Figure 13.2.12: Step 2 - Calculate labor productivity as output divided by labor input.

process_flow.description: Use growth accounting to estimate TFP growth: %ΔA = %ΔY - α%ΔK - (1-α)%ΔL.

image.caption: Figure 13.2.13: Step 3 - Estimate total factor productivity (TFP) growth using the Solow residual method.

process_flow.description: Analyze which factors (innovation, human capital, capital intensity, scale, allocation, management, institutions, trade) are contributing to or hindering productivity growth.
process_flow.mechanism: Compare R&D spending, investment rates, education levels, trade openness, etc., across countries or over time.

image.alt: Diagram: Drivers of productivity (innovation, human capital, capital, scale, allocation, management, institutions, trade).
image.caption: Figure 13.2.14: Step 4 - Identify the drivers of productivity growth to understand why productivity is high or low.

process_flow.description: Compare productivity levels and growth rates with peer countries to identify gaps and potential policy responses.

image.caption: Figure 13.2.15: Step 5 - Compare productivity levels and growth rates across countries to identify gaps and inform policy.

image.alt: Master framework: Collect data → calculate labor productivity → estimate TFP → identify drivers → compare across countries.
image.caption: Figure 13.2.16: Complete framework for analyzing productivity and its role in economic growth.

summary: Productivity—the amount of output produced per unit of input—is the fundamental driver of long-run economic growth and rising living standards. Labor productivity (output per hour) is the most common measure; total factor productivity (TFP) measures the efficiency with which capital and labor are used (the Solow residual). Growth accounting decomposes output growth into contributions from capital, labor, and TFP: %ΔY = %ΔA + α%ΔK + (1-α)%ΔL. TFP growth captures technological progress, innovation, and efficiency improvements. Sources of productivity growth include technological innovation, human capital, physical capital (subject to diminishing returns), economies of scale, resource allocation, management practices, institutions, and trade openness. Canada's labor productivity is about 75-80% of the US level, a persistent gap driven by lower R&D spending, lower capital intensity, internal trade barriers, weaker competition, smaller market size, and lower management quality. Closing the gap would significantly raise Canadian living standards. Policies to boost productivity include R&D incentives, investment tax credits, internal trade liberalization, competition policy reform, management training, and skilled immigration. Understanding productivity is essential for analyzing why some countries grow faster than others and for designing policies to promote long-run prosperity.
